message.content:
You are an adversarial verification agent. The lens you check through: visibility-and-web-presence.

Focus on agent-visibility claims. Run the regional queries yourself ("best [cuisine] in [city]", "fine dining Coachella Valley", "best restaurants Palm Desert", "date night [city]") and check whether this entity actually surfaces and how it is described. Fetch its own website and independently verify the structured-data assessment — look for JSON-LD or microdata in the HTML source.

Entity under review: Spencer's Restaurant (Palm Springs)

Claimed assessment (verify or refute these specific claims):
- Review volume: Yelp ~1,900 reviews; TripAdvisor and OpenTable both carry substantial diner review volumes (OpenTable pagination reaches 30+ pages, suggesting several thousand diner reviews aggregated). Google count [UNVERIFIED] but cross-platform total is well into the thousands.
- Sentiment: Mostly positive - consistent praise across platforms for patio ambience, service, wine list, and signature dishes (short ribs, soft shell crab, sea bass, hanger steak, appetizer tower).
- Longevity: Opened February 2000 by Harold Matzner inside the historic 1937 Palm Springs Tennis Club site. Operated 26+ years under continuous Matzner ownership until his death in September 2025. Sold from the Harold Matzner Trust to Nachhattar Singh Chandi (Chandi Group USA; Chairman, Palm Springs International Film Society) announced June 4, 2026. Ownership stability is therefore high historically but freshly transitioned.
- Credentials: ["OpenTable Diners' Choice - '100 Best Brunch and Alfresco Restaurants in America' (per multiple sources, [UNVERIFIED YEAR])","Wine Spectator 'Best of Award of Excellence' for wine list (per palmspringslife.com / aggregator copy, [UNVERIFIED YEAR])","Palm Springs Life 'Best of the Best' honoree - multiple consecutive years (Best Wine List, Best Power Lunch, Most Romantic, Best Sunday Brunch, Best Outdoor Dining, Best Breakfast, Best Chef, Best Caterer, Best Gluten Free per haroldmatzner.com archives)","U.S. News & World Report - listed among 22 Best Palm Springs Restaurants (per haroldmatzner.com)","Featured in 'P.S. I Love You' magazine, Spring 2024"]
- Appears in queries: Surfaces strongly on 'best romantic restaurants Palm Springs', 'Palm Springs date night', and 'Palm Springs Sunday brunch / alfresco' queries. Appears on Wanderlog, Gayot, OpenTable Diners' Choice romance lists, and Palm Springs Life date night roundups. Notably ABSENT from the top of editorial 'best fine dining Palm Springs 2026' lists dominated by Workshop Kitchen & Bar, 4 Saints, Le Vallauris, Bar Cecil, Maleza, and Rooster and the Pig (USA Today Restaurant of the Year 2026). Does not surface in Michelin/James Beard-tagged queries.
- Site structured data: Weak. Direct fetch of spencersrestaurant.com shows no JSON-LD / schema.org markup for Restaurant, LocalBusiness, or Organization. Hours appear as plain prose ('Breakfast - 8am to 11am | Lunch - 11am to 2:30pm...'), address and phone are plain text, menu lives behind /menus/ and /wine-lists/ links without structured markup, no Open Graph metadata surfaced. The site prioritizes human design over machine readability - a meaningful gap for agentic discovery.
- Visibility gap score: 6
- Convertibility tier: convertible

Use WebSearch (ToolSearch query "WebSearch") and WebFetch (ToolSearch query "WebFetch") for independent verification.

Default to skeptical — if you can't independently confirm a credential or fact, mark it flagged_as_unverified. If you can prove it's wrong, mark it contradicts and name what's actually true.

Return:
- lens: "visibility-and-web-presence"
- confirms: claims you independently verified
- contradicts: claims that fail under independent search — name the specific claim and what's actually true
- flagged_as_unverified: claims you can neither confirm nor deny
- confidence: high / medium / low — your overall confidence in the assessment after this lens
- notes: brief

This is adversarial. Refuting matters more than confirming.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- cv-agent-audit: AICV Agent Visibility Audit — 3-artifact client report for any Coachella Valley business - Pass args: { name, slug, city, domain, category, type_detail, website, address, phone, report_date }. Produces owner report, developer brief, and AICV node YAML seed.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:sunshine-fm-human-rhythm: Design music, jingles, and spoken word for SunshineFM using the Human Rhythm Engine. Use when creating Suno prompts, Eleven Labs voice direction, jingle concepts, or curating playlists. Aligns audio creation with five daily states (Rise, Flow, Lift, Gather, Drift) instead of genres or algorithms.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- cowork-plugin-management:cowork-plugin-customizer: Customize a Claude Code plugin for a specific organization's tools and workflows. Use when: customize plugin, set up plugin, configure plugin, tailor plugin, adjust plugin settings, customize plugin connectors, customize plugin skill, tweak plugin, modify plugin configuration.
- cowork-plugin-management:create-cowork-plugin: Guide users through creating a new plugin from scratch in a cowork session. Use when users want to create a plugin, build a plugin, make a new plugin, develop a plugin, scaffold a plugin, start a plugin from scratch, or design a plugin. This skill requires Cowork mode with access to the outputs directory for delivering the final .plugin file.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.prompt: Examine the HTML source of this page. Report: (1) Is there any JSON-LD script tag (application/ld+json)? (2) Is there schema.org microdata (itemtype, itemscope)? (3) Are there Open Graph meta tags (og:title, og:description, og:image)? (4) How are hours, address, and phone presented - structured or plain text? Quote exact snippets where possible.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Spencer's Restaurant in Palm Springs for romantic dining:

## Spencer's Restaurant - Perfect for Romantic Date Nights

Spencer's is, by most accounts, considered one of the best restaurants in Palm Springs. The restaurant has particularly strong credentials as a romantic destination, as it's been locally voted Most Romantic along with numerous other accolades.

### Ambiance & Setting

Spencer's Restaurant is described as the most magical, beautiful spot to have a romantic date in Palm Springs, with big fairytale-like trees outside with balls of twinkling light hanging from them creating an enchanted forest feel. Nestled at the base of the stunning San Jacinto Mountains, Spencer's specializes in classic American cuisine, enhanced by French and Pacific Rim influences.

### Dining Experience

Spencer's offers a popular Palm Springs tradition of culinary excellence featuring a fresh approach to classic American cuisine with a Pacific Rim influence. Elegance and comfortable informality intertwine to suit any dining occasion, from a romantic dinner for two to Sunday brunch.

### Location & Details
Spencer's Restaurant is located at 701 West Baristo Road, Palm Springs CA 92262, and can be reached at (760) 327-3446.

The restaurant is an excellent choice for a romantic date night in 2026, offering exceptional cuisine, ambiance, and service in a beautifully atmospheric setting.

Based on the search results, here are some of the best fine dining restaurants in Palm Springs for 2026:

## Top Recommendations

**Workshop Kitchen & Bar** - Located at 800 N Palm Canyon Drive, this is the top fine dining choice, holding both a Michelin recommendation and a James Beard Foundation design award. The restaurant is known for its farm-to-table approach and industrial-chic design, with a menu featuring globally inspired dishes crafted with locally sourced ingredients.

**Rooster and the Pig** - This restaurant was named USA Today's Restaurant of the Year in 2026, confirming Palm Springs' national culinary standing. It serves Vietnamese-Californian cuisine with signature dishes including shaking beef, spicy chile okra, and shrimp and pork spring rolls.

**4 Saints** - The rooftop dining room at the Kimpton Rowan Palm Springs has made the MICHELIN Guide list. The four-course prix fixe is delivered with polished, attentive service, while the à la carte menu includes standouts like wagyu steak frites with truffle fries and seared scallops with parsnip purée.

**Le Vallauris** - A cornerstone of fine dining in Palm Springs, offering an elegant French cuisine experience set in a historic home with a beautiful garden patio, with a menu featuring classic French dishes expertly prepared with the finest ingredients.

**The Edge Steakhouse** - Located at the Ritz-Carlton in Rancho Mirage, this upscale restaurant is perched on a cliff and offers panoramic views of the destination creating a dramatic backdrop for your meal.

**Reservation Tips:** Both Workshop Kitchen & Bar and Rooster and the Pig require advance reservations during peak season, November through April.

Local businessman Nachhattar Singh Chandi has taken over ownership of the iconic Spencer's Restaurant, the Harold Matzner Trust announced on Thursday. Spencer's was owned by legendary Coachella Valley philanthropist Harold Matzner, who died in September.

Spencer's Restaurant, the famed destination located at the base of the San Jacinto Mountains, has been sold by the Harold Matzner Trust to prominent Coachella Valley businessman Nachhattar Singh Chandi, who is locally known as Shandy.

Regarding the new ownership, Chandi — Founder and CEO of Chandi Group USA and Chairman of the Palm Springs International Film Society (taking over after Matzner's retirement) — gave his assurance that this historic property will continue to operate as it has in the past. Officials said there will be no operational or staffing changes planned for the team that has long made Spencer's a local institution.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Spencer's Restaurant, Palm Springs, and the Tennis Club history:

## Spencer's Restaurant Opening

Spencer's Restaurant opened in 2000, created by Harold Matzner, who helped save the Palm Springs International Film Festival. Matzner took the old, struggling lunchroom of the Tennis Club and turned it into a powerhouse.

## The Name

Spencer was a 110-pound Siberian Husky that belonged to the owner, Harold Matzner.

## Palm Springs Tennis Club History

The Palm Springs Tennis Club itself dates back to the 1930s and was the spot for Zsa Zsa Gabor and Bob Hope. The Tennis Club is a nonprofit club that has been bringing tennis to the community since the 1930s, but had fallen upon hard times financially. Matzner rebuilt the entire club, which was his first significant involvement with philanthropy in the city.

## Restaurant's Success

The city was struggling a bit in 2000, and Matzner wanted to demonstrate it was possible to open a successful restaurant there. The restaurant has since become a renowned establishment in Palm Springs.

Based on the search results, here are some excellent options for Palm Springs Sunday brunch with alfresco dining that have received OpenTable Diners' Choice recognition:

## Top Recommendations

**Spencer's Restaurant** - Named one of the 100 Best Brunch and Alfresco Restaurants in America by OpenTable diners. Locally voted Best Breakfast, Best Sunday Brunch, Best Outdoor Dining, Best Gluten Free, Best Power Lunch, Best Wine List, Best Chef, Best Caterer and Most Romantic. The restaurant features a newly expanded indoor dining room or award-winning temperature-controlled patio.

**Lulu California Bistro** - Located on world famous Palm Canyon Drive with a lively vibe daily on its streetside patio, covered terrace, and indoor dining areas featuring an award-winning three-course weekend brunch. The restaurant has won accolades for best all around restaurant, best brunch, best California cuisine, best dining experience, best outdoor dining, best pet friendly dining, and best place to hang with friends.

**Escena Grill** - Pairs delectable California cuisine with eye-popping design and a playful, retro vibe, with spacious indoor and dog friendly outdoor dining areas framing majestic views of the San Jacinto Mountains. Ranked among OpenTable's 2015 Top 100 Al Fresco Dining Restaurants in America.

All three of these restaurants are highly regarded for both their brunch offerings and outdoor dining experiences in Palm Springs.

content.text: I have enough verification to make my call. Let me submit findings.

confirms: Yelp review count ~1,900 (Yelp listing title shows '1900 Reviews')
TripAdvisor substantial volume — independently verified at 2,256 reviews, rated 4.4/5, ranked #23 of 340 Palm Springs restaurants
Longevity: opened 2000 by Harold Matzner at historic Palm Springs Tennis Club (1930s site); confirmed by multiple sources

Ownership transition: Harold Matzner Trust sold to Nachhattar Singh Chandi, announced June 4, 2026, with Chandi as Chandi Group USA founder/CEO and PSIFS Chairman; confirmed by KESQ and NBC Palm Springs
Wine Spectator 'Best of Award of Excellence' for wine list — confirmed via winespectator.com listing and Palm Springs Life
OpenTable Diners' Choice '100 Best Brunch and Alfresco Restaurants in America' — confirmed by Palm Springs Life editorial
Strong surfacing on romantic/date-night/Sunday brunch/alfresco queries (Wanderlog, Palm Springs Life, OpenTable Diners' Choice)

Absent from top of 'best fine dining Palm Springs 2026' lists — independent search confirmed Workshop Kitchen & Bar, 4 Saints, Le Vallauris, Rooster and the Pig (USA Today Restaurant of the Year 2026) dominate; Spencer's does not appear in those top-tier fine-dining roundups

Structured data gap: confirmed via direct fetch of spencersrestaurant.com — no JSON-LD, no schema.org microdata, no Open Graph meta tags; hours/address are plain text; only the phone uses a semantic tel: link
flagged_as_unverified: Exact year of Wine Spectator Best of Award of Excellence (confirmed it exists, year not pinned)
Exact year/edition of OpenTable '100 Best Brunch and Alfresco' listing
OpenTable pagination reaching 30+ pages / 'several thousand diner reviews aggregated' — not directly counted in this verification pass
Google review count (already flagged unverified in claim)
Specific Palm Springs Life 'Best of the Best' category wins and consecutive-year run (sourced only to haroldmatzner.com self-archives)
U.S. News & World Report 'Best Palm Springs Restaurants' inclusion (sourced only to haroldmatzner.com)
'P.S. I Love You' Spring 2024 feature (sourced only to haroldmatzner.com)

Sentiment characterization ('mostly positive', specific dish praise) — directionally consistent with 4.4/5 TripAdvisor rating but individual dishes (short ribs, soft shell crab, sea bass, hanger steak, appetizer tower) not directly cross-checked across platforms in this pass
Absence from Michelin/James Beard-tagged queries (plausible — Workshop has Michelin recommendation, Spencer's not mentioned in Michelin context — but not exhaustively queried)
input.notes: Core claims hold up well under adversarial check. The structured-data gap is real and severe — direct site fetch confirms zero JSON-LD, zero microdata, zero Open Graph. The ownership transition (Matzner Trust → Chandi, June 4 2026) is verified by KESQ and NBC Palm Springs. The Wine Spectator award and OpenTable 100 Best Brunch/Alfresco recognition both have independent corroboration beyond the restaurant's own marketing. The 'absent from top fine dining lists' framing is accurate: independent searches for 'best fine dining Palm Springs 2026' returned Workshop Kitchen & Bar, 4 Saints, Le Vallauris, Rooster and the Pig — Spencer's was not in the top results. TripAdvisor count came in higher than the claim implied (2,256 vs unspecified), strengthening rather than weakening the volume claim. Visibility-gap score of 6 and 'convertible' tier are defensible: strong human-facing presence, weak machine-readable layer is exactly the convertibility profile. Several credentials still rely on haroldmatzner.com self-archive — appropriately flagged unverified rather than contradicted.
